mappings.rationaleAI: Art history studying painting, sculpture, and visual arts from ancient times through contemporary art addresses the art of sculpture (A), diverse kinds (B), and the history of Western sculpture (C.1).
Art theory examining the nature of the arts addresses elements of design and principles of composition (A.1) and iconography of sculpture through meaning and interpretation (A.2).
Modern art covering avant-garde movements including Tatlin and Oldenburg addresses representational sculpture (B.2.a) and nonrepresentational sculpture (B.2.b). History of Western sculpture (C.1).
Contemporary art covering the global art scene and art market addresses the history of Western sculpture in its most recent phase (C.1).

Renaissance art covering Michelangelo's sculpture addresses materials of sculpture including stone (A.3), representational sculpture including human figures and devotional images (B.2.a), and the history of Western sculpture (C.1).
Egyptian sculpture, painting, and decorative objects address materials of sculpture (A.3), representational sculpture (B.2.a), and commemorative sculpture including monuments and tombs (B.3.c).
Dada and Surrealism covering readymades, found objects, and performance art address sculpture in the round and environmental sculpture (B.1.a) and the history of Western sculpture (C.1).
The avant-garde covering Picasso and institutional contexts addresses the history of Western sculpture (C.1).
Christian art covering devotional images, crucifixion, and saints addresses representational sculpture including devotional images (B.2.a) and ceremonial and ritualistic objects (B.3.a).
Classics covering Greek and Roman sculpture and temple architecture addresses materials (A.3), representational sculpture (B.2.a), and the history of Western sculpture (C.1).
Classical mythology covering the divine pantheon and hero narratives in art addresses the iconography of sculpture (A.2) and representational sculpture (B.2.a).
Italian Futurism covering visual arts and artistic innovation addresses the history of Western sculpture in the early 20th century (C.1).
Etruscan civilization covering tomb paintings, sculpture, and jewelry addresses representational sculpture (B.2.a), primitive and folk sculpture (B.4), and the history of Western sculpture (C.1).
Prehistory spanning early human material culture addresses primitive and folk sculpture (B.4).
Archaeology covering material evidence from Lascaux to Easter Island addresses materials of sculpture (A.3) and primitive and folk sculpture (B.4).
Materials science covering metals, ceramics, and polymers addresses materials of sculpture (A.3).
